data.title: SOL Braces for 6% Swing as Whales Unload Millions in SOL Before Critical Jobs Data
data.description:
Solana's SOL (SOL-USD) could rise or fall by nearly 6% in the next 24 hours, according to volatility forecasts. That's the warning flashing from volatility trackers as whales unload millions in tokens ahead of key U.S. jobs data expected Friday.

Whales Offload as SOL Price Stalls

Blockchain data from Lookonchain showed several large holders unstaked and sold roughly $46.3 million worth of SOL early Friday. While that's only 0.97% of the day's $4.7 billion trading volume, whale sell-offs often raise red flags. They tend to drag on price action, especially when combined with weak sentiment.

So far, though, the reaction has been muted. SOL is holding steady near $116 after dipping to $112 on Thursday. It's been on a broader downtrend since peaking at $295 in January.

Volatility Index Points to a Sharp Price Move

Traders are now watching Solana's one-day implied volatility index from Volmex, which sits at an annualized 109.70%. That translates to an expected 5.74% swing in either direction within the day. Not massive, but still meaningful — and enough to keep short-term traders on their toes.

This level of volatility isn't out of character. According to CoinDesk, SOL has seen several daily moves of 6% or more since early March. But with Friday's macro backdrop, that range could expand fast.

Markets Watch Payroll Data to Set Crypto Direction

The next big catalyst? The U.S. non-farm payrolls report. Set for release at 12:30 GMT, the report is expected to show 130,000 jobs added in March — down from 151,000 in February and well below the 12-month average of 162,300, per FactSet.

If the numbers come in soft, it could reignite bets on interest rate cuts. That would likely be bullish for risk assets, including crypto. But if the labor market proves too strong, traders might need to brace for the opposite.

At the time of writing, Solana is sitting at $116.37.

data.author: Annika Masrani

data.title: Tesla Stock (TSLA) Jumps on Rumors Musk Will Ditch DOGE Role
data.description:
Tesla (TSLA) stock is climbing again, and it has nothing to do with deliveries. Investors are betting that CEO Elon Musk might finally be stepping back from his controversial DOGE role in Washington — and back toward his day job in Austin.

Tesla Stock Rises Despite Weak Delivery Numbers

On Wednesday, Tesla stock jumped 5.3% even though the company missed Wall Street's Q1 delivery target by about 40,000 vehicles. The total came in at 337,000 — nearly 50,000 fewer than the same time last year. Then on Thursday, shares plunged 5.5% after Donald Trump's surprise tariff bombshell sent markets spinning.

But as of early Friday trading, the stock bounced 0.6% to $268.90, holding firm while broader indexes like the S&P 500 and Dow futures dipped. According to Politico, the driver behind Tesla's rebound isn't cars — it's Musk.

DOGE Exit Rumors Fuel Hopes Musk Refocuses on Tesla

News that Musk might "end his DOGE career early" sparked investor optimism, even though the White House dismissed the Politico report as "garbage." Still, Wall Street seems to believe a pivot away from politics could help Tesla regain its footing.

"With Musk spending considerable time helping the U.S. government, the market may have questioned if Musk was distracted from his role in leading Tesla," said Morningstar analyst Seth Goldstein, who values the stock at $250.

Wall Street Splits on How Much It'll Matter

Wedbush analyst Daniel Ives called the shift "very bullish for Tesla" and maintained his $450 price target. Guggenheim's Ronald Jewsikow went full Eminem, writing, "Guess who's back? back again. Elon's back," and called the narrative "a clear positive."

Still, he rates shares Sell with a $170 target.

Some fund managers have been selling Tesla stock since the election, according to Bank of America's Savita Subramanian. But if Musk really is coming home, some of that money might come back too.

And that's what Tesla shareholders — and maybe car buyers — are now counting on.

Is Tesla a Buy, Sell, or Hold?

Turning to Wall Street, analysts have a Hold consensus rating on TSLA stock based on 16 Buys, 11 Holds, and 12 Sells assigned in the past three months. Furthermore, the average TSLA price target of $312.00 per share implies 16.7% upside potential.

data.title: TikTok Faces U.S. Ban as Trump Races to Strike a Deal to Keep it Alive
data.description:
TikTok could go dark in the U.S. this weekend. The Trump administration is working on a last-minute deal to avoid a national ban, with the official deadline hitting Saturday. According to Fox, Vice President JD Vance said on Thursday that the White House is "working hard on a deal" and an announcement could come before time runs out.

Trump Preps Decision as Deadline Closes In

A 75-day extension granted by Trump in January allowed TikTok to keep operating past its original shutdown date. But now the clock is ticking again. Vance attended a Wednesday meeting to consider final purchase proposals. One person familiar with the matter told Barron's that Amazon has submitted a bid, while AppLovin also expressed interest in a filing with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission.

The stakes are sky-high. TikTok has over 170 million U.S. users, and unless its Chinese parent ByteDance sells to a U.S.-approved buyer, the app could be banned under a law signed last year by former President Joe Biden. Lawmakers argue TikTok collects harmful data. The company denies this.

Experts Clash Over Whether TikTok Will Go Dark

Things could go either way. Evan Horowitz, CEO of advertising agency Movers+Shakers, told Barron's there's a real chance TikTok "could go dark again for a period of time" while a deal or delay gets sorted. He said users were logged out around 11pm EST last time, so a similar blackout could happen again on Friday night.

Others are less convinced. Mark Weinstein, author of Restoring Our Sanity Online, told Barron's he believes a deal announcement is coming, and that would "trigger a 90-day extension and therefore TikTok will not go dark." Business attorney Camron Dowlatshahi added he's not sure a real deal is even on the table, but said he expects another extension while talks continue.

TikTok Charges Ahead in Europe Despite U.S. Chaos

Even as U.S. lawmakers threaten a shutdown, TikTok is ramping up in Europe. According to Reuters, TikTok Shop will launch in France, Germany, and Italy next week. Big names like Carrefour (FR:CA) and AboutYou (DE:YOU) are already onboard. Jan Wilk, head of TikTok Shop UK, said, "This time, we're moving faster."

Whatever happens in the U.S., TikTok clearly isn't slowing down elsewhere.

What Is the Best Social Media Stock to Buy?

For investors interested in investing in the Social Media sector, we have rounded up the best stocks to buy in this sector, using the TipRanks Stocks Comparison tool. Analysts are most bullish on Meta (META) and Pinterest (PINS), both of which carry a Strong Buy rating. Among the two, Pinterest offers the highest upside potential from its current price.

data.title: Coinbase Fires Shot to Launch XRP Futures in U.S.
data.description:
Coinbase (COIN) just filed to launch regulated XRP (XRP-USD) futures in the U.S., even though traders are still betting against the token. In a move announced April 3, Coinbase Institutional said it’s submitted paperwork to the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) to self-certify monthly XRP futures contracts under the symbol XRL. Each contract will represent 10,000 XRP, settled in U.S. dollars. If all goes to plan, they'll go live April 21.

Coinbase Targets Regulated XRP Futures Launch

Coinbase said it's working with futures commission merchants and market participants who back the launch. “We're excited to announce that Coinbase Derivatives has filed with the CFTC to self-certify XRP futures — bringing a regulated, capital-efficient way to gain exposure to one of the most liquid digital assets,” the firm said in its announcement.

The contracts will be margined, cash-settled, and track XRP's spot price. As a safety buffer, trading halts if XRP swings more than 10% in an hour. That's a pretty tight leash for a crypto asset known for its volatility.

Coinbase isn't first to the punch here. In March, Bitnomial, a Chicago-based exchange, launched what it called the "first-ever CFTC-regulated XRP futures in the U.S." Meanwhile, overseas, XRP futures are already standard on big players like Binance, OKX, Bybit, and BitMEX.

Negative Funding Rates Signal Bearish Mood

Still, the market doesn't seem thrilled. Despite Coinbase's big swing, traders are leaning heavily short. According to CoinGlass, XRP derivatives funding rates remained negative as of April 4 — meaning short-sellers are paying long traders to stay in the game. That's usually a telltale sign of bearish conviction.

Funding rates act like balancing payments in perpetual futures. If longs are bullish, they pay shorts. If shorts dominate, it flips. Right now, it’s the latter. And it's not just a blip — rates have stayed in the red since late March.

Coinbase is betting there's demand for regulated XRP exposure in the U.S. But with traders doubling down on short positions, it might be a bumpy debut.

Is COIN Stock a Good Buy?

Analysts remain cautiously optimistic about COIN stock, with a Moderate Buy consensus rating based on 11 Buys and nine Holds. Year-to-date, COIN stock has decreased by more than 30%, and the average COIN price target of $330.79 implies an upside potential of 93.7% from current levels.

data.title: Stock Market News Today, 4/4/25 – Futures Decline amid Tariff-Induced Market Volatility
data.description:
U.S. stock futures continued to drop on Friday morning, reflecting market concerns after President Trump announced new tariffs, which led to a sell-off in U.S. equities yesterday. Futures on the Nasdaq 100 (NDX), the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA), and the S&P 500 (SPX) were down 0.43%, 0.84%, and 0.66%, respectively, at 4:23 a.m. EST, April 4.

The major indices suffered substantial losses on Thursday. The Dow Jones, the S&P 500, and the Nasdaq Composite (NDAQ) declined about 4%, 4.8%, and 6%, respectively. The Dow and the S&P 500 both saw their largest single-day losses since June 2020, while the Nasdaq faced its worst day since March 2020.

Also, the Russell 2000, which represents small-cap stocks, has entered a bear market with a drop of over 20% from its recent peak.

Further, the Magnificent Seven stocks were particularly hard-hit, losing over $1 trillion in combined market value.

The market reacted strongly to Trump’s plan for a 10% tariff on all imports, with higher rates for some countries. This raised fears of retaliation by other nations, which could hurt global trade and slow down growth. Also, Trump's recent signals about openness to trade negotiations have increased market uncertainty.

Investors are now turning their attention to the March Jobs report for insights about the health of the U.S. labor market. This data will influence the Federal Reserve’s monetary policy decisions. Economists expect 140,000 new nonfarm payrolls and an unchanged unemployment rate of 4.1%.

Meanwhile, the U.S. 10-year treasury yield was down, floating near 3.948%. Simultaneously, WTI crude oil futures are trending lower, hovering near $65.30 per barrel as of the last check.

Elsewhere, European indices opened lower today as investors grappled with the impact of the recently announced U.S. tariffs.

Japanese Market Ended Lower on Friday

Japan's stock indices fell sharply on Friday, mirroring Wall Street’s plunge. Investors across the region remain cautious as global markets face the economic fallout of Trump's aggressive trade measures.

Japan's Nikkei and Topix indices closed lower by 2.75% and 3.37%, respectively.

Investors should note that the Hong Kong and China markets were closed today for the Qingming Festival.

Interested in more economic insights? Tune in to our LIVE webinar.

data.author: Radhika Saraogi

data.title: Trump's Tariffs Wipe Out $3.1 Trillion as U.S. Stocks Suffer Worst Drop Since 2020
data.description:
U.S. stocks experienced a massive sell-off, erasing approximately $3.1 trillion in market value, the largest single-day loss since March 2020. This downturn followed President Donald Trump’s announcement of sweeping new tariffs, sparking fears of a global trade war and potential recession.

The impact was widespread, with major indexes posting significant losses. The S&P 500 (SPY) sank nearly 5%, Nasdaq 100 (QQQ) dropped nearly 6%, while the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA) plummeted 1,679 points, marking a 4% drop. Investors scrambled to assess the fallout, with uncertainty rippling across global markets.

Tech Sector Bears the Brunt

Technology companies, heavily reliant on international supply chains, were among the hardest hit.

To start with, Apple (AAPL) alone lost $311 billion in market cap, plummeting 9% after President Donald Trump announced a 54% tariff on Chinese imports, crippling its supply chain.  At the same time, Amazon and Meta each declined by 9%. Microsoft (MSFT) and Nvidia (NVDA) each tumbled over 7%, dragging the Nasdaq into freefall. The “Magnificent Seven” tech giants collectively lost approximately $1 trillion in market capitalization.

Investors Seek Safe Havens amid Volatility

The U.S. dollar weakened, with the WSJ Dollar Index experiencing its sharpest decline since 2023. Additionally, oil prices dropped more than 6%, reflecting concerns over reduced global demand. At the same time, JPMorgan analysts raised their forecast of the likelihood of a recession to 60% after the new tariffs were announced.

In response to the market turmoil, investors are rushing to safer bets. U.S. Treasuries are in high demand, pushing yields lower as traders look for stability. Gold, a go-to safe haven, initially surged to a record high of $3,167.57 per ounce, but later gave up some gains as investors scrambled for cash.

Many are also holding more cash, waiting for the storm to pass. At the same time, hedge funds that bet against stocks are raking in profits. If the uncertainty drags on, more money could flow into defensive assets, putting even more pressure on stocks.

Which Magnificent 7 Stock Is the Best?

We used the TipRanks Stock Comparison Tool for Magnificent 7 Stocks to understand which stock is most favored by analysts. Investors can consider investing in one of these stocks after thorough research.

Currently, Nvidia, Microsoft, Amazon (AMZN), and Meta Platforms (META) score Wall Street's "Strong Buy" consensus rating, with NVDA stock offering the highest upside potential.

data.author: Shalu Saraf

data.title: Apple iPhone for $2,300? Trump's Tariffs Could Push Up iPhone Prices by 43%
data.description:
America's favorite Apple (AAPL) iPhones are set to get pricier, thanks to President Donald Trump's sweeping reciprocal tariffs. Rosenblatt Securities analyst Barton Crockett calculated the impact of the tariffs and found that Apple's most advanced iPhone to date, the 16 Pro Max, could cost as much as $2,300, significantly higher than its current price of $1,599. Crockett says that tariffs could push up iPhone prices by almost 43%, if Apple is to pass on all the additional duties to end consumers.

Despite being an American icon, Apple manufactures the majority of its iPhones in China. It's no surprise that AAPL stock was the worst hit from the "Liberation Day" tariff announcements. AAPL stock dropped 9.3% yesterday, wiping out nearly $300 billion from its trillion-dollar market capitalization, and marking the worst trading day for the stock since March 2020.

Crockett's Compelling Calculations

Apple sells over 200 million iPhones each year, with major markets being the U.S., China, and Europe. The analyst’s calculation shows that Apple will need to raise prices for iPhone and Apple Watch by 43% to offset the increased tariffs, while iPad will become 42% dearer, and Macs and AirPods could become 39% costlier. Crockett ranks 1,059 of the 9,446 analysts ranked on TipRanks. He has a success rate of 54% and an average return per rating of 5.70%.

Apple's smartphone sales have started declining recently in its major markets, as customers remain dissatisfied with Apple Intelligence features. Consumers are not upgrading to newer iPhones at the same rate as they did earlier. At such a crucial time, these tariffs could hit Apple where it hurts the most. Crockett suggests that the bloated tariffs could cost Apple nearly $40 billion. He also thinks that Apple CEO Tim Cook could try to lobby the President for some exemptions on its China manufacturing, like he did during Trump's first tenure. However, negotiations could turn uglier this time, as Trump seems committed to enforce the tariffs.

Trump imposed the highest tariffs on China, set at 54%. The President appears determined to reduce reliance on China's low-cost manufacturing capabilities and promote the "Make in America" dream. Recently, Apple has tried to shift a part of its manufacturing to India and Vietnam, yet it has not been able to shift the focus from China completely. Notably, both India and Vietnam have also been levied with 26% and 46% tariffs, respectively.  

CFRA Research analyst Angelo Zino believes that Apple will have a tough time to pass on more than 5% to 10% of the increased cost to consumers. He added that Apple will likely hold on to any price increases until its planned launch of the iPhone 17 series this fall. At the same time, Apple's tariff crisis could open a sweet-spot for rival Samsung (SSNLF), since South Korea faces lower tariffs than China.

Is AAPL a Good Stock to Buy?

Wall Street remains divided on Apple's stock trajectory amid the ongoing challenges. On TipRanks, AAPL stock has a Moderate Buy consensus rating based on 17 Buys, 11 Holds, and four Sell ratings. Also, the average Apple price target of $250.71 implies 23.4% upside potential from current levels. In the past year, AAPL stock has gained 20.9%.

See more AAPL analyst ratings

data.title: Apple Stock Faces "Risk of Demand Destruction in the U.S.," Says Top Analyst
data.description:
Apple Inc. (AAPL) has lost over $300 billion in market value, marking its worst drop since March 2020. The sell-off comes as President Donald Trump's latest tariffs hit Apple hard, imposing a steep 54% duty on Chinese imports. While Apple has been diversifying its supply chain, Raymond James analyst Srini Pajjuri warns that the company is still facing "significant reciprocal tariffs," with India-made products also subject to a 26% duty. The added costs, the Top analyst says, could lead to demand destruction in the U.S.

It is worth noting that Pajjuri ranks 885 out of more than 9,441 analysts tracked by TipRanks. He has a success rate of 54%, with an average return per rating of 14.9% over a one-year timeframe

Raymond James Warns of a Demand Crisis for Apple

Pajjuri warns that new tariffs could slash Apple's 2025 earnings by up to 25%. With over 90% of its hardware built outside the U.S., the analyst believes that Apple may have to raise U.S. hardware prices by 30% to offset costs or absorb the hit, which would squeeze profits.

The analyst sees a real risk that higher prices will drive customers away, dampening demand in Apple's largest market. He also doesn't rule out retaliatory tariffs from China, which could further hurt Apple's global sales. For now, Raymond James is keeping its estimates unchanged, waiting to see how long the tariffs last and how Apple navigates the challenge.

What's Next for Apple Stock

Apple has been shifting production to India and Vietnam, but escalating tariffs there are making things even more difficult. If China retaliates, Apple could lose a key revenue stream in one of its most important international markets. According to Main Street Data, Greater China contributed $18.5 billion of Apple's December quarter revenues. So, if China takes serious retaliatory measures, Apple’s sales could be at risk.

With Wall Street on edge, investors are watching Apple's next move and bracing for what could be more pain ahead.

Is AAPL Stock a Buy?

The stock of Apple has a consensus Moderate Buy rating among 32 Wall Street analysts. That rating is based on 17 Buy, 11 Hold, and four Sell recommendations assigned in the last three months. The average AAPL price target of $250.71 implies 23.39% upside from current levels.

See more AAPL analyst ratings

data.title: Biggest Tax Hike Since 1968 Fuels Recession Fears, Says JPMorgan
data.description:
JPMorgan (JPM) is sounding the alarm. The firm warns that President Donald Trump's latest tariffs, announced on Liberation Day, amount to the largest tax increase in the U.S. since 1968. While these tariffs are not direct taxes, they raise the cost of imported goods, driving up prices for consumers. The bank cautions that the economic strain from higher costs could push the U.S. closer to a recession.

The stock market has already shown signs of distress. The S&P 500 (SPY) fell by 4.9%, while the Nasdaq 100 (QQQ) dropped nearly 6%, erasing billions of dollars in market value. Looking to commodities, gold prices have declined by 1%, cooling the metal’s recent rally, while oil prices have dropped nearly 8% amid concerns over new tariffs and increased production from OPEC+ members. 

How Big Is the Impact?

JPMorgan said that the average U.S. tariff rate has now climbed to its highest level in over a century, increasing by 22%. Economists argue that this sharp rise could slow economic growth and place a financial burden on American households.

As businesses face higher import costs, they are likely to pass these expenses on to consumers, making everyday items like cars, electronics, and household goods more expensive.

Rising Fears of a Recession

JPMorgan has raised its estimate of a global recession from 40% to 60%, citing the risks posed by these tariffs. Analysts said retaliatory tariffs by other countries will make things worse. For instance, Jeep maker Stellantis (STLA) has stopped production at its Mexico and Canada plants. Meanwhile, Volkswagen has added an "import fee" on U.S. shipments, paused rail deliveries from Mexico, and halted some European imports. At the same time, major retailers like Walmart (WMT), Best Buy (BBY), and Target (TGT) are warning of price hikes.

Making things worse, business sentiment in the U.S. is slipping, and supply chain issues are piling up. As costs rise and trade tensions heat up, companies are feeling the pressure. Markets have tumbled, fueling fears of a looming recession.

What Is the Price Target for JPM?

Turning to Wall Street, JPM stock has a Moderate Buy consensus rating based on 11 Buys and seven Holds assigned in the last three months. The average price target for JPM stock is $274.38, suggesting a potential upside of 19.98% from the current level. Shares of the company have lost 4.1% year-to-date.

data.title: JPM, BAC, GS: Bank Stocks Crater as U.S. Recession Risk Accelerates
data.description:
It wasn’t just technology stocks that crashed on April 3. Bank stocks took it on the chin too.

Stocks of leading U.S. banks and brokerages fell as much as 10% as the prospect that the U.S. economy will enter a recession this year accelerate. The KBW Nasdaq Bank Index ended the trading day down 10% after U.S. President Donald Trump announced baseline tariffs of 10% on nearly all imports into the country.

Large consumer banks were among the hardest hit financial institutions, with Bank of America's (BAC) stock falling 11%. Shares of Wells Fargo (WFC) and JPMorgan Chase (JPM) decreased 9% and 7%, respectively. Trump's tariffs were higher than many on Wall Street expected, and they have exacerbated concerns about weak economic growth.

Brokerages Fall Too

A weaker U.S. economy prompted by Trump's bombshell new tariffs may hurt merger and acquisition activity as businesses cut back on investments in the face of uncertainty. As such, shares of Wall Street investment bank Morgan Stanley (MS) dropped 9.5% on April 3. Rival Goldman Sachs (GS) saw its stock fall 9% amid volatile trading.

Brokerages and wealth management firms also got pummeled by the tariff news. Falling equity markets may mean diminished revenue generated from fees on assets under management. It also likely means less stock trading. Consequently, Robinhood Markets (HOOD) and Interactive Brokers (IBKR) saw their stocks fall 9% and 5.8%, respectively.

Is GS Stock a Buy?

Goldman Sachs stock has a consensus Moderate Buy rating among 15 Wall Street analysts. That rating is based on 10 Buy and five Hold recommendations assigned in the last three months. The average GS price target of $648.23 implies 26.84% upside from current levels.

Read more analyst ratings on GS stock
